question_text:
Hi, I just transferred to the Zurich branch this morning and my setup is completely broken.
First, when I dial external vendor numbers on the desktop app, the line is dead silent—I can't hear them at all, though they say they hear me.
Second, I'm locked out of the 'ERP_Upgrade' directory on the intranet; it just says I don't have permission, which makes no sense since I viewed it yesterday.
Finally, I tried to send a scanned contract from the hallway printer to a vendor's email. The printer screen showed a green checkmark, but the vendor never received the email. Please fix my account!
answer: Recommended classification: second_level_support Reasoning (summary) - The ticket describes three distinct, non-trivial failures: one-way audio on an external call from a desktop telephony app, unexpected loss of access to an intranet directory, and "scan to email" from a networked printer reporting success but the recipient never receives mail. Taken together these symptoms match past incidents that required engineering investigation and cross-team changes (network/firewall, AD/group and authentication configuration, print/scan infrastructure), not routine first-level fixes. This supports escalation to second-level support [Data: Sources (2, 22, 28, 11)]. What the indexed records explicitly document (relevant examples) - Printer/scan delivery failures were caused by network segmentation / missing firewall rules between servers and printer VLANs; fixing firewall/routing according to the print standard restored function [Data: Sources (22)]. - Incorrect access, defaults or permissions that appeared after an organizational or configuration change were traced to AD group membership and GPO mapping; correcting group membership fixed the issue [Data: Sources (2)]. - Printing failures after a Windows update and VM restore were caused by broken network share settings on the print server; disabling/re-enabling the share (and restoring VMs) allowed print jobs to be processed again [Data: Sources (28)]. - Authentication/connectivity issues stemming from incorrect authentication server (ADFS) client parameters required parameter adjustments on ADFS (engineer action) to restore connectivity - showing similar authentication/permission issues can require second-level configuration changes [Data: Sources (11)]. Why this ticket should be handled as second_level_support - The three failures likely span multiple technical domains (telephony/VoIP stack, AD/permissions/authentication, and networked printer/service delivery) and mirror past incidents where fixes required engineering actions: firewall/routing changes, AD/GPO corrections, ADFS parameter updates, or VM/print-server restore operations - all of which were handled by engineers in the referenced tickets rather than by routine front-line steps [Data: Sources (22, 2, 28, 11)]. Recommended next steps (these are my recommendations, not items documented in the records) - Escalate to second-level / engineering and include Network, AD/Identity, and Print/Device teams. - Ask the user for immediate context: name of the telephony client and version, exact intranet path and username, printer model and scan job ID/timestamp. - Have engineering check (a) VoIP call logs and client audio settings, (b) the AD permissions/group memberships and recent changes affecting the ERP_Upgrade directory, and (c) the print/scan server logs and SMTP relay/firewall rules for the scanner-to-email path. If you want, I can draft the exact escalation note including the relevant symptom list and which teams to include.

question_text: URGENT MAJOR INCIDENT: We are getting flooded with calls from the Sales and Finance teams after the HR department updated the organizational chart in the system last night. First, our freelance consultants are complaining they are locked out of their shared workspaces and get an error screen. Second, the sales reps are trying to call clients, but the dialer just beeps and drops the call immediately. Finally, the finance team in Zurich is furious because their confidential invoices are physically printing out in the Basel office. What on earth did the org chart update break?
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Reasoning (records-based): - The SharePoint/permissions symptom (freelancers locked out of shared workspaces after an org change) closely matches a documented incident where a reorganization and new AD/group structure caused partial access loss and inconsistent behavior. That case required escalation to the M365/Identity engineering team for in‑depth analysis (unique permissions vs. sensitivity labels, direct group membership tests, and System Engineer involvement) [Data: Sources (30)]. - The printer symptom (invoices for Zurich printing in Basel) mirrors a documented incident where an organizational/group change caused incorrect default-printer assignments via GPOs. The root cause was inconsistent AD group membership after a reorganization; resolution required correcting group membership and updating GPO assignment (gpupdate/test/login) according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard [Data: Sources (2)]. - The current ticket reports multiple, different subsystems failing (collaboration permissions, telephony/dialer, and printing). Past multi-domain incidents (printing outages that required infrastructure or OS/VM restores and coordination across teams, or network/VLAN/firewall fixes for printers) involved specialist teams (network, core engineering, or system engineers) rather than only first-line fixes [Data: Sources (28, 22)]. What the records explicitly document - Reorg/group changes can cause SharePoint access issues that were not resolved by first-line steps and were escalated to M365/Identity system engineers for detailed analysis (ticket TI-1578891) [Data: Sources (30)]. - Incorrect default printer assignment after organizational changes was traced to AD group membership and fixed by adjusting group membership and GPOs (ticket TI-1623017) [Data: Sources (2)]. - Print outages have required network/firewall adjustments or VM/OS restores and coordination with network/core teams (tickets TI-1622483 and TI-931293) [Data: Sources (22, 28)]. Recommended (not documented in the records; my recommendation) - Escalate to second-level teams immediately: Identity/M365 (for workspace access), Telephony/UC engineering (for dialer), and Print/Core Infrastructure (for cross-site printing). This recommendation is based on the multi-domain nature of the failures and the precedent that similar incidents required engineer involvement. If you want, I can list the most relevant prior ticket numbers and their documented root causes and remediation actions to attach to your escalation.

question_text:
Hi IT, our compliance audit team flagged something on one of our project folders a couple of weeks ago and asked us to make some changes to it. We did the changes — at least we thought we did — and now things have been weird ever since, in confusing ways for different groups of people.

The most frustrating part is who is affected. Some of the external partners we work with on the project tell us they can sometimes open the folder and see the files, and then later in the same day they cannot. They say nothing has changed on their side — same browser, same network, same password as always. Then on our side, two of our regular team members say they can see the folder name in their file list but when they click any document inside, it just will not open or they get blocked. The files used to open fine for them just last month.

We have a specific user in finance who said her access keeps flickering: she had access in the morning, lost it around lunch, got it back briefly after restarting her laptop, then lost it again. Her manager already checked everything and her account looks completely normal.

And separately — though we cannot tell if it is connected — a totally different folder used by a different team has stopped appearing in their file list at all. They can still get to it if they have the URL bookmarked, but it is like the folder has gone invisible for some of them.

People are blaming us, the audit team, the project lead, and even the IT contractor who did some work last month. Everyone has a theory. What is actually going on, and how do we get this under control before someone blames the wrong people?
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Reasoning (explicitly documented similarities) - Your symptoms - intermittent/"flickering" access for some users, some users able to see a folder but not open files, bookmark/URL access working while folder is invisible in lists, and inconsistent behaviour across internal vs external users - match multiple prior incidents that required engineering-level investigation rather than a simple first-line fix. For example, a SharePoint folder that behaved inconsistently after being renamed and then encrypted with a Sensitivity Label was diagnosed as a conflict between SharePoint ACLs and AIP encryption plus token caching; resolving it required label removal/reapplication, alignment of groups and token invalidation by engineers (TI-1578891 / continuation) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. - Another incident with similar intermittent visibility/access was caused by token caching combined with Conditional Access/device compliance delays; the fix required Conditional Access policy adjustment and re-authentication (TI-1571046) [Data: Sources (38)]. - A separate case where external users lost access sporadically was due to Azure AD Connect synchronizing an on‑prem group that had the same name as an existing cloud group (different object IDs); remediation required consolidation of groups, cleaning the on‑prem group, and a full sync (TI-1583327) [Data: Sources (13)]. - A further example showed that incorrect group nesting in Entra ID caused broad access denial until engineers corrected the nesting and cleaned up permission groups (TI-1569482) [Data: Sources (20)]. What the records explicitly show (facts from past cases) - Root causes in similar tickets included: mismatch between SharePoint permissions and AIP/Sensitivity Label encryption, token-caching / stale claims, Conditional Access evaluation delays, Azure AD Connect synchronization overwriting cloud groups, and incorrect group nesting [Data: Sources (31, 38, 13, 20)]. - Remediation actions actually performed in those incidents included: removing and reapplying the Sensitivity Label and aligning the label's permitted group with the SharePoint ACL, invalidating/forcing token renewal and adjusting Conditional Access policies, consolidating group structures and performing a full Azure AD Connect sync, and correcting Entra ID group nesting. These were done by system engineers / Identity teams and resulted in stable access after monitoring [Data: Sources (31, 38, 13, 20)]. Classification decision - Because the ticket's symptoms closely mirror multiple documented incidents that required escalation to identity/M365 or network/security engineering teams and non‑trivial configuration/remediation (label/group alignment, syncs, policy changes, token invalidation), classify this as second_level_support. The past tickets listed above show that resolving such mixed and intermittent access problems was not resolved by routine first-line steps alone and needed engineering intervention [Data: Sources (31, 38, 13, 20)]. Recommended next steps (these are my recommendations; where similar steps were used in past cases I cite them) - Escalate to the M365 / Identity engineering team (in past incidents the BU Infrastructure - M365 / Identity group and system engineers handled comparable cases) [Data: Sources (30, 31, 13)]. (Recommended) - Have engineers check for Sensitivity Label / AIP encryption applied after permission changes, and verify whether the label's allowed group differs from the SharePoint ACL; past remediation included removing/reapplying the label and aligning the group references (worked previously) [Data: Sources (31)]. (Recommended) - Review Azure AD group origins (cloud vs on‑prem) and Azure AD Connect behavior to ensure object IDs and memberships are correct and that no cloud group was overwritten by a synchronized on‑prem group - consolidation and a full sync resolved similar external-access issues before [Data: Sources (13)]. (Recommended) - Investigate token caching and Conditional Access/device compliance timing: force token invalidation and re-authentication for test users, and review CA policies that might intermittently block access; in a prior case CA policy adjustment and re-authentication stopped the flicker [Data: Sources (38)]. (Recommended) - Verify group nesting in Entra ID to rule out ineffective inheritance; prior fixes involved correcting nesting and removing deprecated groups (TI-1569482) [Data: Sources (20)]. (Recommended) If you want, I can map which internal teams to loop in based on the past tickets (Identity/M365, Entra/AD Sync, and - if Sensitivity Labels are implicated - Information Protection / Purview). I relied only on the indexed incident records cited above to form this classification and the examples used.

question_text: Hi IT, we've had a strange situation develop over the past two weeks where multiple unrelated-looking issues have surfaced across different parts of the organization. We cannot tell if these are connected to one underlying change or multiple separate things. First, when people try to open our internal HR portal or other internal-only websites in their browsers, they get a big warning page about not being able to trust the connection. They have to manually click through to bypass it. Internal websites only — public sites work fine. Second, our home-working population (about 80 people) say their secure work-from-home connection refuses to authenticate. The connection software says it cannot verify the server identity. They were working fine until a couple of weeks ago, and nothing changed on their laptops. Third, our office wireless network has been showing as unavailable for newer team members and people who recently got replacement laptops — the network just does not show up in their list of available networks at all. Their laptops are correctly registered in our standard management system. Fourth, when people try to set up their work email on iPhones — particularly people who switched phones recently — the setup goes around in a loop. The email app starts, then shows a message about device approval, then drops back to the start. The context we have: our infrastructure team did some kind of internal maintenance about three weeks ago, and our security team tightened some internal rules around the same time. We cannot tell if these are causing all four problems, two separate causes, or whether something else is going on. We need an analysis.
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Summary reasoning - The ticket describes multiple distinct failures that map closely to prior incidents which required engineer-level troubleshooting and remediation: internal browser certificate trust errors after a PKI renewal (TI-1700005), VPN authentication breaks tied to a changed VPN/server certificate template (TI-1700006), and Wi‑Fi profile not deploying due to Intune/dynamic-group assignment scope (TI-1700010). Each of those past cases was handled by infrastructure/PKI/network/endpoint engineers rather than being resolved by first‑line helpdesk procedures [Data: Sources (55, 104, 87, 13)]. - Because at least two of your described symptoms (internal TLS trust failures and VPN server identity validation failures) in the knowledge base were caused by PKI/template changes and required coordination between PKI and network teams, the consolidated symptom set in your ticket is consistent with an issue set that needs second‑level (engineer/escalation) involvement rather than routine first‑line fixes [Data: Sources (55, 104)]. What the records explicitly document (relevant parallels) - Internal-only sites showing browser trust warnings after an issuing CA renewal; resolution required publishing the intermediate CA correctly to AIA/CRL distribution points and validation by infrastructure engineers (KF80 TI-1700005) [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN clients failing to authenticate after a PKI change because the new server certificate template omitted a required EKU; resolution required reissuing or adjusting certificates/templates and deploying corrected certs (KF80 TI-1700006) [Data: Sources (104)]. - Corporate Wi‑Fi not appearing on certain devices because the Wi‑Fi profile was targeted via a dynamic Intune group tied to device compliance state; the assignment scope was adjusted by endpoint engineers to restore connectivity (KF90 TI-1700010) [Data: Sources (87)]. - Certificate/Identity issues on domain controllers and LDAP-S failures have previously required PKI or DC certificate re-enrollment actions by infrastructure teams (KF80 TI-1700008) [Data: Sources (13)]. Recommended next steps (these are recommendations and are not directly documented in the cited records) - Escalate this ticket to your PKI/identity and network/endpoint engineering teams for joint investigation (recommended). - Provide the engineers with timelines of the maintenance/changes, samples of browser certificate chains from affected internal sites, a VPN negotiation log for a failing connection, and representative device names showing missing Wi‑Fi profiles (recommended). If you want, I can draft a short escalation note that summarizes the parallels to the past incidents and lists the specific artifacts to collect for the engineers.

question_text:
Hi IT, our team did some kind of internal infrastructure maintenance over a weekend a couple of weeks ago — something behind the scenes that wasn't supposed to affect anyone visibly. Since then we have been getting a series of reports from different parts of the company that look unrelated but all started around the same time.

First, when people try to open our internal HR system in their browsers — to look at their records or report sick days — their browsers throw a big warning page saying they cannot trust the connection. They have to click through some 'advanced' option to actually get to the page. Same thing on a couple of other internal websites that everyone normally uses.

Second, our remote workers who work from home or while travelling — about 80 people — say they cannot get their secure work-from-home connection established anymore. Their connection software just refuses to authenticate. The error mentions something about not being able to verify the server identity. They were working fine until this maintenance work happened, and nothing changed on their laptops.

Third, the leadership team uses a special configuration for their email correspondence so that confidential communications can be cryptographically verified — outgoing messages they send carry a kind of seal, and they can read sealed messages they receive from external partners. That whole feature stopped working. They cannot apply the seal to outgoing email anymore, and they cannot read sealed email they receive. They were given new credentials around the time of the maintenance work, but those credentials do not seem to do anything.

Fourth, several of our internal business applications — the HR app, our SharePoint installation, and our network access control system — report that they can no longer reach our central user-account system through the protected channel they normally use. They CAN reach it through the unprotected channel, but our security policy does not allow operating that way.

These all started in the same window. Different teams are pointing at each other — the infrastructure team that did the maintenance, the application owners, the security team. We need an actual answer about what is happening.
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Why: the symptoms in this ticket (multiple unrelated services suddenly showing TLS / certificate failures, VPN mutual-authentication breaking, and protected email/sealing no longer working) closely match prior incidents that were caused by PKI/CA work and required engineer-level remediation rather than routine first‑line fixes. What the records explicitly document - Internal HTTPS services showing browser trust warnings after a PKI change: a past case where an issuing CA was renewed over a weekend produced NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID across internal sites; the resolution required publishing the intermediate CA to AIA/CRL and validation by engineers (TI-1700005) [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN authentication failing for many remote users after the weekend PKI work: a prior incident where the new VPN server certificate was issued from a refreshed template that omitted a required EKU; resolution involved reissuing certificates from a corrected template and deploying them via GPO (TI-1700006) [Data: Sources (104)]. - LDAP-S / AD secure-bind failures tied to domain controller certificates after PKI/template changes: a prior ticket where DC certificates were re-enrolled from the internal CA after an auto-enrollment template fix, restoring LDAP‑S functionality (TI-1700008) [Data: Sources (13)]. These three examples show the same pattern: a PKI/CA change caused multiple, cross‑service failures and each required engineering changes to CA/template/publishing and certificate re-issuance or re-enrollment - actions that were performed by infrastructure/PKI engineers in those tickets [Data: Sources (55, 104, 13)]. Classification rationale (concise) - The scope (many services across HTTPS, VPN, AD, and secure email) and the timing (all started after an infrastructure/PKI maintenance window) strongly indicate a centralized PKI/CA issue rather than separate routine user-level problems. - In the indexed cases with the same symptoms, resolution required engineer action (template fixes, CA publishing, certificate re-issuance and GPO deployment), not first-level troubleshooting steps. Therefore this should be handled as second_level_support [Data: Sources (55, 104, 13)]. Recommended (not from records) - Escalate immediately to the PKI/Infrastructure engineering team and open a second‑level incident (if not already done). - Have engineers verify whether CA/issuing certificates were renewed/published to AIA/CRL distribution points, review certificate templates (EKUs and auto-enrollment settings), and plan coordinated re‑enrollment/re‑issuance for affected server and user certificates. - Coordinate with application owners to validate service trust chains after fixes and schedule GPO / client distribution as needed. If you want, I can draft a short incident summary you can send to the PKI engineers that highlights the matching prior cases and the likely areas to check.

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Classification: second_level_support Reasoning (comparison to past cases) - The symptom where migrated users can sign into cloud services but on-prem applications report "user not found / not recognized" matches a documented UPN-suffix / provisioning error that required reversing on-prem userPrincipalName changes and running a full Azure AD Connect sync to restore on‑prem authentication (TI-1700001). This was not a simple helpdesk fix in the record but required identity‑team changes to AAD Connect/provisioning [Data: Sources (22)]. - The inconsistent group memberships (on‑prem shows new groups while cloud still shows old groups) matches a case where the new group objects were placed in an OU outside the AAD Connect sync scope; the fix was to update the AAD Connect OU filter and perform a full sync (TI-1700002) - an infrastructure change handled by engineers [Data: Sources (54)]. - The built-in mobile clients failing while Outlook mobile worked corresponds to a tenant-level Modern Authentication enforcement issue that required switching clients to modern auth and re-enrolling devices (TI-1700003) - again handled by engineers/endpoint team and not a pure first-line password reset [Data: Sources (4)]. - Scheduled tasks run by service accounts failing due to authentication aligns with a case where service accounts were unintentionally targeted by a tenant Conditional Access policy requiring MFA; resolution involved creating an exclusion group and adjusting CA/AAD Connect handling (TI-1700004). That required changes to CA configuration and AAD Connect scope by the identity/infra team [Data: Sources (50)]. Because your ticket contains multiple issues that in the indexed records were each resolved by identity/infra engineers performing configuration changes to Azure AD Connect, Conditional Access, device enrollment/modern-auth settings, and on‑prem attribute remediation, it aligns with second_level_support rather than a routine first‑level fix. What the records explicitly document (actions actually taken in similar incidents) - TI-1700001: reverted affected on‑prem userPrincipalName values to the original suffix and triggered a full Azure AD Connect synchronization; validated users regained access to on‑prem apps [Data: Sources (22)]. - TI-1700002: updated AAD Connect OU filter to include the new OU containing migration group objects and performed a full sync so group memberships propagated correctly to the cloud [Data: Sources (54)]. - TI-1700003: resolved mobile client failures by moving clients to modern authentication and re‑enrolling affected devices in Intune; documented the consequence of tenant modern-auth enforcement for built-in mail clients [Data: Sources (4)]. - TI-1700004: created a KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA exclusion group, added service accounts, updated the Conditional Access policy to exclude that group, and reviewed AAD Connect sync scope; validated scheduled tasks resumed [Data: Sources (50)]. Recommended next step (this is a recommendation, not a record-backed action) - Recommend escalating this ticket to the identity/infra engineering team (second level) to (a) review AAD Connect provisioning/OU and UPN handling for the second migration wave, (b) examine Conditional Access targeting and exclude non-interactive service accounts if needed, and (c) assess tenant modern-auth impact on built-in mobile clients and coordinate re-enrollment - all actions consistent with the fixes documented above. (Recommendation only - not pulled verbatim from the records.) If you want, I can prepare a short list of the specific records above to attach to the escalation for the engineers.
